  metadata contract with tests.

## Open questions

- Is there a single `Model` union, or per-helper model types? (Leaning:
  discriminated union with shared metadata.)
- How are formats expressed — a format token enum, or an Intl-style descriptor?
- Does dashboard own number/date formatting, or only _describe_ it for the
  renderer? (Leaning: describe, don't format.)

## Acceptance criteria

- Each helper maps a real `@sisal/analytics` result into a typed semantic model
  with complete metadata (label/type/unit/format/role/series/trend as
  applicable).
- The package has **zero** runtime dependency on any renderer or UI framework
  (enforced by a dependency test).
- Docs show the **same** `timeSeries()` model consumed by **two** different
  renderers (e.g. a Recharts adapter and a custom one), proving agnosticism.

## Affected packages

New `@sisal/dashboard`; depends on `@sisal/core` + `@sisal/analytics` types
only. No `@sisal/orm` dependency; no UI dependency.

## Required examples / tests

- For each helper: an analytics-result → model mapping test asserting the
  semantic metadata.
- A docs example: one analytics time series → `dashboard.timeSeries()` → two
  renderer adapters.
- A dependency-direction test (no UI/renderer imports).

## Dialect compatibility notes

Dashboard is dialect-agnostic — it maps result sets, not SQL. Any dialect
limitation is inherited from the analytics query that produced the result and is
out of scope here.
```

### docs/v0.13.0-roadmap.md

URL: https://gilvandovieira.github.io/sisal/v0.13.0-roadmap.html

```md
---
title: v0.13+ Roadmap
---

# Sisal v0.13+ roadmap — DuckDB / External OLAP Engine Investigation

DuckDB is attractive — but only **after** the analytical IR exists. v0.13+ is an
**investigation** of DuckDB (and other OLAP engines) as a later **compiler /
execution target** for the analytics IR built in
[v0.7](v0.7.0-roadmap.md)/[v0.8](v0.8.0-roadmap.md)/[v0.11](v0.11.0-roadmap.md).
It is explicitly **not** the first analytics milestone and is **not a
requirement** for any earlier ETL/analytics work.

## Status legend

| Symbol | Meaning                                                    |
| ------ | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
| 🔴     | Not started · 🟡 In progress · 🟢 Done — move to CHANGELOG |

## Goal

Determine whether, and how, the Sisal analytics IR should compile to DuckDB (and
similar engines) for analytical workloads the Postgres-first path serves poorly
— without making DuckDB a dependency of the core stack.

## Scope — investigate

- **DuckDB as an analytical execution backend** (in-process OLAP).
- **DuckDB as a compiler target** from the existing analytics IR (the IR's
  `executionPreference` already anticipates this seam — see v0.7).
- **Querying external / local data** (files, attached databases).
- **Parquet / Arrow** possibilities (columnar in/out).
- **Extracting from Postgres/Neon into analytical execution** (read OLTP →
  analyze in DuckDB) — **ELT**-style workflows.
- **Performance benchmarks** vs. Postgres pushdown on representative analytics.
- **Deno compatibility** of DuckDB bindings; **native binding risks**;
  **deployment tradeoffs** (binary size, cold start, serverless viability).

## Why it comes after the IR (not before)

The analytics IR is the abstraction that lets Sisal target _more than one_
engine. Building DuckDB-first would couple analytics to one engine and bypass
the Postgres-first model most apps need. The IR (v0.8) must be the source of
truth; DuckDB is one of its backends.

## Non-goals

DuckDB is **not** required for v0.10 (ETL) or v0.11 (analytics). No DuckDB
dependency is added to `@sisal/core`/`orm`/adapters. No commitment to ship a
DuckDB backend — this milestone may conclude "not yet / not worth it."

## Risks

- **Native binding fragility on Deno** — DuckDB bindings may be immature or
  FFI-heavy; a hard dependency could hurt portability (and the Node/npm reach
  from v0.6).
- **Two-engine semantics drift** — analytics that behave differently on Postgres
  vs DuckDB. The IR + capability gates must contain this.
- **Premature optimization** — adopting DuckDB without benchmark evidence of a
  real Postgres-pushdown shortfall.

## Open questions

- Is DuckDB an **embedded** backend, an **attached** analytical store, or an
  **ELT scratch** engine? (Could be all three; pick the first use case.)
- Does compiling to DuckDB need the **transformable AST** deferred in v0.8, or
  can it compile from the same fragment IR with a DuckDB dialect?
- Parquet/Arrow: in scope here, or a separate ingestion concern (v0.14+)?
- Serverless/Deploy viability of a native engine.

## Acceptance criteria

- A written **DuckDB investigation report**: feasibility, IR-compilation sketch,
  benchmark results vs Postgres pushdown, Deno/Node binding risk assessment, and
  a go/defer/reject recommendation per use case.
- A prototype (behind a flag, not shipped) compiling one analytics IR query to
  DuckDB **only if** the benchmarks justify continuing.
- A clear statement that no earlier milestone depends on DuckDB.

## Affected packages

Investigation against `@sisal/core`/`@sisal/analytics` (a potential future
`@sisal/duckdb` backend); no required changes to existing packages.

## Required examples / tests

- Benchmark harness comparing an analytics query on Postgres pushdown vs DuckDB.
- A prototype IR→DuckDB compilation fixture (flagged), if pursued.

## Dialect compatibility notes

DuckDB would be a **new dialect/execution target** behind the analytics IR's
`executionPreference`, not a replacement for the pg/neon/sqlite/libsql adapters.
The Postgres-first analytics path remains the default.
```

### docs/v0.14.0-roadmap.md

URL: https://gilvandovieira.github.io/sisal/v0.14.0-roadmap.html

```md
---
title: v0.14+ Roadmap
---

# Sisal v0.14+ roadmap — Optional Native/Rust Acceleration Investigation

The last milestone on the line, and the most conditional. v0.14+ investigates
native/Rust acceleration **only if** benchmarks prove a real bottleneck that
**neither SQL pushdown nor DuckDB** ([v0.13+](v0.13.0-roadmap.md)) can solve
cleanly. There is no default assumption that Sisal ever ships native code.

## Status legend

| Symbol | Meaning                                                    |
| ------ | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
| 🔴     | Not started · 🟡 In progress · 🟢 Done — move to CHANGELOG |

## Goal

Decide, on **benchmark evidence**, whether a specific hot path justifies native
(Rust) acceleration — and if so, scope the smallest possible native surface.

## The gate (non-negotiable)

> **Do not introduce Rust/native complexity without a benchmark-backed reason.**

Native code is considered **only after** SQL pushdown (Postgres) and DuckDB have
been measured and shown insufficient for a _specific, reproduced_ bottleneck.
The first deliverable of this milestone is the benchmark, not the binary.

## Scope — potential areas (each must be benchmark-justified)

- **Large local Parquet processing**
- **Arrow / vectorized transformations**
- **Compression / decompression hot paths**
- **Very large CSV / JSON ingestion**
- **Custom native aggregators**
- **High-throughput event ingestion**

## Non-goals

No native code without a reproduced, benchmarked bottleneck; no rewrite of
existing TS/SQL paths that already perform adequately; no native dependency that
breaks Deno/Node portability (the v0.6 runtime reach) without an explicit,
documented tradeoff; no general "rewrite it in Rust" ambition.

## Risks

- **Complexity & portability cost** — native bindings threaten the Deno-first /
  Node-reach story, cross-platform builds, and serverless deploy. This must be
  weighed explicitly per area.
- **Maintenance burden** — a Rust surface is a long-term cost; only worth it for
  a durable, proven bottleneck.
- **Premature adoption** — the central risk the gate exists to prevent.

## Open questions

- Which bottleneck (if any) survives the v0.13+ DuckDB investigation? DuckDB may
  already cover Parquet/Arrow/vectorized work, removing the reason for Rust.
- If native is justified, what's the integration surface — Deno FFI, a Wasm
  module, a sidecar process?
- How is the native path kept optional so the pure-TS install always works?

## Acceptance criteria

- A **benchmark report** for any candidate area showing SQL pushdown **and**
  DuckDB are insufficient for a reproduced workload — or a conclusion that
  native acceleration is **not** justified (an equally valid outcome).
- If pursued: a narrowly-scoped native prototype behind a flag, with the pure-TS
  path remaining the default and the portability tradeoff documented.

## Affected packages

Investigation only; potentially a future optional native helper consumed by
`@sisal/etl`/`@sisal/analytics`. No required changes to the core stack.

## Required examples / tests

- A reproducible benchmark harness for each candidate hot path.
- If pursued: parity tests proving the native path matches the TS/SQL result.

## Dialect compatibility notes

Out of scope — native acceleration concerns local data processing
(Parquet/Arrow/ ingestion), not the SQL dialects. Any native path must not
regress the portability guarantees established in v0.6 (Deno + Node) or the
four-dialect support.
```

### docs/v0.4.0-roadmap.md

URL: https://gilvandovieira.github.io/sisal/v0.4.0-roadmap.html

```md
---
title: v0.4.0 Roadmap
---

# Sisal v0.4.0 roadmap

This document captures the API gaps surfaced while building the
[`examples/neon-hot-feed`](../examples/neon-hot-feed/README.md) example — a
Reddit-style hot feed on Neon/PostgreSQL that must stay Deno Deploy / Neon-HTTP
friendly. Each gap forced the example into a raw-SQL escape hatch. None of them
are bugs; they are missing conveniences that, if added, would let real
applications stay inside Sisal's typed surface instead of dropping to strings.

Treat this as the working backlog for the `0.4.0` cycle. Every item lists the
concrete workaround the example uses today (with file references), a proposed
API, the affected packages, and acceptance criteria. Where an item maps onto the
Drizzle surface, the [drizzle-parity](drizzle-parity.md) row to move is noted so
the two documents stay in step.

## Status legend

| Symbol | Meaning                                |
| ------ | -------------------------------------- |
| 🔴     | Not started                            |
| 🟡     | In progress / partial                  |
| 🟢     | Done — move the entry to the CHANGELOG |

## Priority summary

| # | Item                                              | Priority | Rough effort | Status |
| - | ------------------------------------------------- | -------- | ------------ | ------ |
| 1 | Typed database-function caller                    | P1       | M            | 🟢     |
| 2 | Serverless-safe SQL migration applier + Neon CLI  | P1       | M–L          | 🟢     |
| 3 | Keyset / cursor pagination helper                 | P1       | M            | 🟢     |
| 4 | Raw expressions in builder `SET` / `VALUES`       | P2       | M            | 🟢     |
| 5 | Richer DDL generation (indexes, CHECK, functions) | P2       | L            | 🟢     |
| 6 | Non-interactive (batched) transaction API         | P2       | M            | 🟢     |
| 7 | Column-name mapping (camelCase ↔ snake_case)      | P3       | M–L          | 🟢     |
| 8 | Temporal date/time semantics                      | P3       | M            | 🟢     |

P1 items together close the "I had to write raw SQL for the core feed/mutation"
story; they should land first.

---

## 1. Typed database-function caller — P1 — 🟢 Done

**Shipped.** `defineFunction(name, { args, returns })` declares a function's
positional argument column types and its return shape (a `RETURNS TABLE (...)`
column map → typed row, or a single column builder → typed scalar).
`db.call(fn, args)` renders one `select * from <schema.fn>($1::t1, …)` statement
with the `::type` casts taken from the argument column types and every value
bound, then runs it with `.execute()` (rows) or `.one()` (asserts exactly one).
A scalar return renders `select fn(...) as "result"` and unwraps the value. Pure
`@sisal/orm`, no adapter changes. Exports `defineFunction`,
`FunctionDefinition`, `FunctionCall`, `FunctionConfig`, `FunctionArgsConfig`,
`FunctionReturnsConfig`, `FunctionArgsInput`, `FunctionRow`. The example's
`src/vote.ts` now uses it with no `sql` string; covered by
`packages/orm/functions_test.ts` (render + param order, casts, scalar/table,
`.one()`, validation).

**Problem.** The whole point of the example is an atomic vote via the PostgreSQL
function `app.vote_post(post_id, user_id, value)`. There was no Sisal surface
for calling a stored function and mapping its `RETURNS TABLE (...)` to a typed
row, so the call was a hand-written `sql` template and the result type was
asserted by hand.

**Where it surfaced.**

- `examples/neon-hot-feed/src/vote.ts` —
  `` sql`select * from app.vote_post(${postId}::uuid, ${userId}::uuid, ${value}::smallint)` ``
  with a manually declared `VoteResult` interface and `::uuid` / `::smallint`
  casts written into the string.

**Current workaround.** Raw `sql` tag + `db.query<VoteResult>(...)`, plus a
manual "no row returned" guard. Correct and parameterized, but stringly typed
and easy to drift from the SQL signature.

**Proposed API.** A function descriptor + caller that renders a single
`SELECT * FROM fn(args)` statement and infers argument and row types:

```ts
import { columns, defineFunction } from "@sisal/orm";

const votePost = defineFunction("app.vote_post", {
  args: {
    postId: columns.uuid(),
    userId: columns.uuid(),
    value: columns.smallint(),
  },
  returns: {
    id: columns.uuid(),
    score: columns.integer(),
    upvotes: columns.integer(),
    downvotes: columns.integer(),
    hot_score: columns.doublePrecision(),
  },
});

// args are positional-by-declared-order, cast from the arg column types;
// the result is typed from `returns`. Returns rows; a `.one()` helper can
// assert exactly one.
const [row] = await db.call(votePost, {
  postId,
  userId,
  value: -1,
}).execute();
```

The caller must emit casts from the declared arg column types (so `::uuid` /
`::smallint` come from the descriptor, not the string) and bind every argument
as a parameter.

**Affected packages.** `@sisal/orm` (descriptor, renderer, `db.call`). No
adapter changes — it renders to ordinary parameterized SQL.

**Acceptance criteria.**

- `defineFunction` infers an args object type and a row type.
- `db.call(fn, args)` renders `select * from <schema.fn>($1::t1, $2::t2, …)`
  with the casts taken from the arg column types and all values bound.
- A scalar-returning variant (`returns: columns.integer()`) yields a typed
  scalar helper.
- The example's `src/vote.ts` is rewritten to use it with no `sql` string.
- Network-free render test pins the generated SQL and parameter order.

**Open questions.** Named vs positional arguments; `VOID`/`SETOF`/`RECORD`
return shapes; OUT parameters. Start with positional args + `RETURNS TABLE`.

---

## 2. Serverless-safe SQL migration applier + Neon CLI target — P1 — 🟢 Done

**Shipped.** `splitSqlStatements(sql)` is now exported from `@sisal/migrate` (a
dollar-quote/`$tag$`/string/quoted-identifier/comment-aware splitter, hardened
to drop empty/stray semicolons), covered by
`packages/migrate/sql_split_test.ts`; the CLI and the `neon-hot-feed` example
both use it (the example dropped its local copy). The migrator gained a
`splitStatements` apply mode (core `createMigrator`, forwarded through
`createPgMigrator` and `createNeonMigrator`): a string `up`/`down` step is split
and run one statement per `driver.execute(...)` call, still recording history —
`createNeonMigrator` defaults it to `true` (and `useTransaction` to `false`) for
the HTTP transport. The `sisal` CLI gained a **Neon target**:
`sisal init --neon` scaffolds a `dialect: "postgres"` + `provider: "neon"`
config, and `sisal migrate` then applies through `@sisal/neon` over HTTP (one
`INIT_TARGETS` entry + a `provider` discriminator on `MigrateConfig`). Covered
by `packages/migrate/{sql_split,split_apply,cli}_test.ts`.

**Problem.** A `.sql` migration file holds several statements separated by `;`,
but the Neon serverless driver sends each query through PostgreSQL's extended
protocol, which allows exactly one statement per call. Splitting on `;` also
breaks `CREATE FUNCTION ... $$ ... ; ... $$` bodies. Sisal has no
serverless-safe applier, and the `sisal` CLI's migrate path targets the
`postgres` / `sqlite` adapters, not a Neon-HTTP applier.

**Where it surfaced.**

- `examples/neon-hot-feed/src/sql_split.ts` — a hand-rolled, dollar-quote-aware
  statement splitter (handles `$$`/`$tag$`, single-quoted strings, and line /
  block comments).
- `examples/neon-hot-feed/src/migrate.ts` — reads each file, splits it, and
  executes statements one at a time via `db.execute(raw(stmt))`.

**Current workaround.** The two files above, plus idempotent DDL
(`IF NOT EXISTS` / `CREATE OR REPLACE`) so re-runs are safe. There is no
applied- migration history table in the example.

**Proposed API.**

1. A reusable splitter in `@sisal/migrate` (e.g. `splitSqlStatements(sql)`),
   dollar-quote/string/comment aware, with tests.
2. A migration **apply mode** that runs each `.sql` file statement-by-statement
   for drivers that cannot take multi-statement queries (a per-statement vs
   per-file execution flag on the migrator), preserving the history table.
3. A Neon target for the `sisal` CLI so `sisal migrate` works against
   `@sisal/neon` (HTTP) the same way it does for `@sisal/pg`. Adding it should
   be one `INIT_TARGETS` entry plus the adapter wiring, per the CLI's existing
   "one registry entry per database" design.

**Affected packages.** `@sisal/migrate` (splitter + apply mode), `@sisal/neon`
(applier wiring), `packages/migrate/cli.ts` (Neon target + `CliDialect`).

**Acceptance criteria.**

- `splitSqlStatements` is exported from `@sisal/migrate`, covers `$$`/`$tag$`,
  string literals, and comments, and is unit tested (port the example's tests).
- The migrator can apply a multi-statement `.sql` file over a single-statement
  driver without breaking function bodies, still recording history.
- `sisal init --neon` (or equivalent) scaffolds a Neon config and
  `sisal migrate` applies migrations through `@sisal/neon`.
- The example switches to the shared splitter / applier and drops its local copy
  (or keeps it only as a teaching reference, with a pointer here).

**Notes.** `@sisal/pg` over a direct TCP connection already tolerates
multi-statement files (deno-postgres simple protocol). This item is specifically
about the **serverless/HTTP** path that the example optimizes for.

---

## 3. Keyset / cursor pagination helper — P1 — 🟢 Done

**Shipped.** `SelectBuilder.keyset({ orderBy, after, form? })` infers the cursor
type from the `orderBy` columns, emits the keyset comparison against `after`
(omit it for the first page) plus the matching `ORDER BY`, and returns a
`KeysetSelectBuilder` whose `.limit(n).execute()` yields `{ rows, nextCursor }`
(a `nextCursor` only when a full page came back). Two predicate forms: the
default `"expanded"` nested `or`/`and` (works with mixed directions and every
dialect) and `"row-value"` (`(a, b, c) < (x, y, z)`, a uniform sort direction).
To enable this, `asc()`/`desc()` now return an `OrderTerm` (a `Sql` subtype that
also carries the column + direction; backward compatible), and a column's
`propertyName` is a literal type so the cursor keys infer. Precision: ending
`orderBy` with a unique key (e.g. `id`) makes the order total; for date/time
cursors, use DB-returned cursor values because Temporal can represent
nanoseconds, PostgreSQL stores microseconds, and JS `Date` stores milliseconds.
Exports `OrderTerm`, `KeysetOptions`, `KeysetCursor`, `KeysetKeys`,
`KeysetPage`, `KeysetSelectBuilder`. The example's `getNewFeed` (expanded) and
`getHotFeed` (row-value) are both builder-native; covered by
`packages/orm/keyset_test.ts`. Adds the divergence-by-design row (footnote ⁸) to
[drizzle-parity](drizzle-parity.md).

**Problem.** Every feed re-implemented the keyset comparison
`(a, b, c) < (x, y, z)` by hand. The `/new` feed could do it with nested
`or`/`and`, but the `/hot` feed (a three-column keyset over a computed
`double precision` column) was clearer as raw SQL — so it dropped out of the
typed builder.

**Where it surfaced.**

- `examples/neon-hot-feed/src/queries.ts` — `getNewFeed` hand-builds
  `or(lt(created_at, c), and(eq(created_at, c), lt(id, c)))`; `getHotFeed` uses
  a raw `sql` template for the three-column keyset and matching `ORDER BY`.

**Current workaround.** Two bespoke implementations and two cursor shapes
(`NewCursor`, `HotCursor`) defined by hand.

**Proposed API.** A helper that takes an ordering spec and an "after" cursor and
emits both the `WHERE` keyset predicate and the matching `ORDER BY`:

```ts
const page = await db.select().from(posts)
  .where(eq(posts.columns.status, "published"))
  .keyset({
    orderBy: [
      desc(posts.columns.hot_score),
      desc(posts.columns.created_at),
      desc(posts.columns.id),
    ],
    after: cursor, // typed from the orderBy columns
  })
  .limit(20)
  .execute();
// page exposes rows + a `nextCursor` derived from the last row.
```

It should be able to emit either the expanded nested `or`/`and` form or a SQL
row-value comparison `(a, b, c) < (x, y, z)` where the dialect supports it.

**Timestamp-precision caveat.** Temporal can represent nanoseconds, PostgreSQL
timestamps store microseconds, and JS `Date` stores milliseconds. A keyset over
a timestamp tiebreaker can skip or duplicate rows at a page boundary if the
cursor value is re-created at a different precision. Prefer DB-returned cursor
values and always include a unique final key such as `id`.

**Affected packages.** `@sisal/orm` (builder method + cursor inference).

**Acceptance criteria.**

- A `keyset({ orderBy, after })` builder method infers the cursor type from the
  `orderBy` columns and emits the predicate + ordering.
- `nextCursor` is produced from the last row of a full page.
- Render tests pin both the nested-`or` and row-value output forms.
- Documented precision behavior for timestamp tiebreakers.
- The example's `getNewFeed` and `getHotFeed` both become builder-native.

**Drizzle reference.** Drizzle has no first-class keyset helper either; this is
a chance for Sisal to lead rather than match. Add a roadmap row to
[drizzle-parity](drizzle-parity.md) noting the divergence-by-design.

---

## 4. Raw expressions in builder `SET` / `VALUES` / `DEFAULT` — P2 — 🟢 Done

**Shipped.** `.set({...})`, `.values({...})`, and `onConflictDoUpdate.set`
accept a `Sql` expression for any column value alongside literals:

```ts
set({ score: sql`${posts.columns.upvotes} - ${posts.columns.downvotes}` });
values({ id, created_at: sql`now()` });
```

Expressions render **inline** (a column reference renders table-qualified; raw
text passes through) while literal values still bind as parameters. The public
types `InsertValues` / `UpdateValues` widen each column value to `value | Sql`;
`InferSelect` / `InferInsert` are unchanged. Covered by
`packages/orm/set_values_expr_test.ts` (UPDATE, INSERT, and `onConflict` mixing
literals and expressions; param binding verified). Scope is **scalar**
expressions: `UPDATE ... FROM` and `INSERT ... SELECT` remain out of scope, so
the example's `recomputeAggregates` (an `UPDATE ... FROM (subquery)`) stays raw.
`DEFAULT` expressions are a DDL concern tracked under item 5. Moves the parity
row.

**Problem.** The builder could only set/insert bound values, not SQL
expressions. You cannot write
`set hot_score = app.calculate_hot_score(score, created_at)` or insert a column
whose value is a SQL function call, so any computed mutation drops to raw SQL.

**Where it surfaced.**

- `examples/neon-hot-feed/src/seed.ts` → `recomputeAggregates` — a raw
  `UPDATE ... FROM (... LEFT JOIN ...)` whose `SET` clause calls the hot-score
  function, because the builder's `.set({...})` only accepts literal values.

**Current workaround.** Raw `db.execute(raw(\`update ...\`))`.

**Proposed API.** Allow `sql` expressions as values in `.set(...)` and
`.values(...)` (and as column defaults):

```ts
await db.update(posts)
  .set({
    score: sql`${posts.columns.upvotes} - ${posts.columns.downvotes}`,
    hot_score: sql`app.calculate_hot_score(score, created_at)`,
  })
  .where(eq(posts.columns.id, id))
  .execute();
```

`UPDATE ... FROM` / `INSERT ... SELECT` are larger asks; start with scalar `sql`
expressions in `SET`/`VALUES`, which covers the common "compute from other
columns" case.

**Affected packages.** `@sisal/orm` (builders type widening + renderer).

**Acceptance criteria.**

- `.set({...})` and `.values({...})` accept `Sql` expressions alongside literal
  values, with types updated accordingly.
- Expressions render inline and parameterized; literal values still bind.
- Render tests for an UPDATE and an INSERT mixing literals and expressions.

**Drizzle reference.** Drizzle allows `sql` in `.set()` / `.values()`; this is a
parity item. Move the relevant row in [drizzle-parity](drizzle-parity.md).

---

## 5. Richer DDL generation — P2 — 🟢 Done (functions/triggers → v0.5.0)

**Shipped (5.1 indexes + 5.2 CHECK).** Table-level `index()` / `uniqueIndex()`
now carry per-column direction via `asc()`/`desc()` terms, raw `` sql`...` ``
**expression** keys, and a partial-index predicate via a new `.where(predicate)`
method:

```ts
defineTable("posts", cols, (t) => [
  index("hot_feed")
    .where(sql`${t.status} = 'published'`)
    .on(desc(t.hotScore), desc(t.createdAt), desc(t.id)),
  uniqueIndex().on(sql`lower(${t.email})`),
]);
```

`generate{Postgres,Sqlite,Libsql}UpStatements` emit the `col DESC`/`ASC`
ordering, parenthesized expression keys, and a trailing `WHERE`; expression and
predicate text renders portably (table prefix stripped) exactly like CHECK
constraints, which already emitted inline. The serialized index shape is now
structured (`{ value, direction?, expression? }` + optional `where`), so
`SCHEMA_SNAPSHOT_VERSION` is **2** (regenerate snapshots). Adds the public
`IndexColumnSpec` type and the `SisalIndexColumnSnapshot` descriptor. Covered by
the pg/sqlite Drizzle-parity tests and the gated **pg 16/17/18** and **SQLite**
and **libSQL** integration suites
(`rich indexes (DESC / partial / expression) apply`), and the parity index row
moved. **Deferred:** 5.3 functions/triggers (raw `CREATE FUNCTION` / `TRIGGER`
DDL attached to a snapshot) is a larger design moved to the
[v0.5.0 roadmap](v0.5.0-roadmap.md) as its own item.

**Problem.** `generatePostgres/Sqlite/LibsqlUpStatements` emit only additive
`CREATE TABLE` / `ADD COLUMN`. They cannot express DESC index ordering, partial
or expression indexes, CHECK constraints, or functions/triggers. So the example
keeps hand-written `.sql` migrations as the source of truth and treats
`src/schema.ts` as a typed _mirror_ for the builder, not the generator's output.

**Where it surfaced.**

- `examples/neon-hot-feed/migrations/0001_init.sql` — `posts_new_feed_idx` /
  `posts_hot_feed_idx` use `... desc` column ordering and there is a
  `value in (-1, 1)` CHECK; none of this is producible from the snapshot.
- `examples/neon-hot-feed/src/schema.ts` — declares `index(...).on(...)`,
  `primaryKey`, and `check(...)` purely for typing; the DDL is written by hand.

**Current workaround.** Hand-written SQL migrations; the `defineTable` `extras`
metadata is informational only.

**Proposed API / scope (incremental).**

1. **Index ordering + uniqueness/partiality**: let `index()` / `uniqueIndex()`
   carry per-column direction (`asc`/`desc`), a `where` predicate (partial), and
   expression columns, and have the generator emit them.
2. **CHECK constraints**: emit `check(name, sql\`...\`)`into`CREATE TABLE`.
3. **Functions/triggers** (stretch): a way to attach raw DDL fragments to a
   snapshot so generated migrations can include `CREATE FUNCTION` bodies
   verbatim, ordered after table creation.

**Affected packages.** `@sisal/orm` (`table.ts` constraint model +
`createSchemaSnapshot`), each adapter's DDL generator
(`packages/{pg,sqlite,
libsql}/ddl.ts`), and `@sisal/migrate` diff
classification.

**Acceptance criteria.**

- DESC/partial/expression indexes and CHECK constraints declared via
  `defineTable` appear in generated `CREATE` DDL for each dialect.
- Destructive-change detection still holds (new constraints are additive; drops
  go to the `destructive` array).
- The example can regenerate `0001_init.sql` from `src/schema.ts` (functions may
  remain hand-written until item 5.3 lands).
- Move the corresponding [drizzle-parity](drizzle-parity.md) rows.

---

## 6. Non-interactive (batched) transaction API — P2 — 🟢 Done

**Shipped.** `db.batch([...])` runs several pre-built statements (query
builders, `` sql`...` `` fragments, or rendered `SqlQuery`) as one atomic,
**non-interactive** unit and returns one `OrmQueryResult` per statement. It
renders every statement up front (an unbound placeholder throws a clear
`OrmError` before touching the database), then prefers a driver's native `batch`
and otherwise wraps the statements in one `transaction` (`begin; …; commit`),
rolling the whole batch back on any failure. A `batch?` hook was added to the
`OrmDriver` contract and implemented in **every adapter** (`@sisal/pg` — and
therefore `@sisal/neon`, which builds on `createPgOrmDriver` — plus
`@sisal/sqlite` and `@sisal/libsql`), each running the batch in one atomic
transaction. Exports `BatchStatement`. Covered by `packages/orm/batch_test.ts`
(native-batch path, transaction fallback, rollback, unbound-placeholder, mixed
inputs, empty) and the gated `pg`/`sqlite`/`libsql` integration suites. No
statement may depend on a previous one's result — that is what `transaction()`
and database functions are for.

**Problem.** `db.transaction(async (tx) => { ... })` is interactive: it holds a
connection open across every awaited statement in the callback. On Deno Deploy +
Neon HTTP that is the awkward shape the example is built to avoid. There is no
way to express "run these N statements as one atomic, non-interactive
transaction in a single round trip."

**Where it surfaced.** The README's
[Important Neon note](../examples/neon-hot-feed/README.md#important-neon-note)
contrasts interactive transactions, non-interactive transactions,
single-statement mutations, and database functions. The example reaches for a
database function precisely because the middle option does not exist in Sisal.

**Current workaround.** Push multi-step logic into a database function (item 1)
or a single data-modifying statement, sidestepping interactive transactions.

**Proposed API.** A batch that collects pre-built statements and submits them as
one `begin; …; commit` unit (one round trip where the driver supports it):

```ts
await db.batch([
  db.insert(posts).values(p),
  db.update(posts).set({ score }).where(eq(posts.columns.id, id)),
]); // atomic, non-interactive
```

No values may depend on a previous statement's result (that is what interactive
transactions and database functions are for); the batch is for independent
writes that must commit together.

**Affected packages.** `@sisal/orm` (`db.batch`, driver contract addition),
adapters (`@sisal/neon`, `@sisal/pg`, …) implement a batched-execute path; the
HTTP path can use the driver's array/transaction mode.

**Acceptance criteria.**

- `db.batch([...])` renders and submits multiple statements atomically, ideally
  in one round trip on Neon HTTP.
- Failure rolls the whole batch back.
- Clear error if a builder in the batch references an unbound placeholder.
- Documented in the example README's transaction table as the "non-interactive
  transaction" row, with a runnable snippet.

---

## 7. Column-name mapping (camelCase ↔ snake_case) — P3 — 🟢 Done

**Shipped.** `defineTable` now derives physical column names through a naming
strategy that **defaults to `snake_case`** (`hotScore` → `hot_score`, idempotent
on already-snake_case keys). Override per table with the `naming` option
(`"snake_case"` | `"camelCase"` | `"preserve"` | a `(key) => name` function),
process-wide with `setDefaultColumnNaming(strategy)`, or per column with
`.named(...)` (always wins). The builder maps key↔physical on both write
(INSERT/UPDATE/SET/ON CONFLICT) and read (`SELECT *`/`RETURNING *` expand to
`phys AS key` once a table has any renamed column), so code that stays inside
the builder is transparent to the strategy. `InferSelect`/`InferInsert` and
`db.query.<table>` already keyed by the JS property name, so they were
unaffected. Covered by `packages/orm/naming_test.ts` and the
`parity: column casing` test; parity/legacy tables that assert literal SQL opt
out with `naming: "preserve"`. See the [drizzle-parity](drizzle-parity.md)
casing row (footnote ⁷) for the divergence-by-design note.

**Problem.** Sisal used the `defineTable` property key as the physical column
name verbatim. To match the SQL (`hot_score`, `created_at`, `post_id`), the
example's models used snake_case keys, so application code read
`posts.columns.hot_score` instead of idiomatic `hotScore`.

**Where it surfaced.**

- `examples/neon-hot-feed/src/schema.ts` — every column key is snake_case to
  line up with the hand-written DDL and the `app.vote_post` return columns.

**Current workaround.** Snake_case keys throughout TS.

**Proposed API.** Optional explicit column names and/or a table-level naming
strategy, so TS can stay camelCase while SQL stays snake_case:

```ts
const posts = defineTable("posts", {
  hotScore: columns.doublePrecision("hot_score"), // explicit name, or…
  createdAt: columns.timestamp({ withTimezone: true }),
}, { naming: "snake_case" }); // …a table strategy
```

The renderer already resolves `column.definition.name ?? propertyName`, so an
explicit-name path is a small change; a naming strategy is the larger, ergonomic
win. Result-row mapping (DB `hot_score` → JS `hotScore`) must be handled on read
for the strategy to be transparent.

**Affected packages.** `@sisal/orm` (column factories accept a name;
`defineTable` naming strategy; result mapping), with knock-on effects for
inference and `db.query.<table>` relational helpers.

**Acceptance criteria.**

- A column can declare an explicit physical name distinct from its key.
- An opt-in table/global naming strategy maps camelCase keys to snake_case
  columns for both rendering and result mapping.
- Inference (`InferSelect` / `InferInsert`) reflects the JS-side key names.
- The example can switch to camelCase keys with no SQL changes.

**Drizzle reference.** Drizzle supports explicit column names
(`integer("snake_case")`) and a `casing` option. This is a parity item; add/move
the row in [drizzle-parity](drizzle-parity.md).

---

## 8. Temporal date/time semantics — P3 — 🟢 Done

**Shipped.** Sisal v0.4.0 treats Temporal as the preferred date/time model.
`columns.date()` infers `Temporal.PlainDate`, `columns.time()` infers
`Temporal.PlainTime`, `columns.timestamp()` infers `Temporal.PlainDateTime`, and
`columns.timestamp({ withTimezone: true })` infers `Temporal.Instant`. Explicit
`mode: "date"` and `mode: "string"` keep legacy JS `Date` or raw string
behavior. SQL parameters normalize Temporal values to ISO strings before
adapters see them, including arrays.

PostgreSQL DDL now maps `timestamp` to `timestamp` and `timestamptz` to
`timestamptz`; existing instant columns should use
`columns.timestamp({ withTimezone: true })`. SQLite/libSQL keep date-like values
as `TEXT`. Result parsing remains opt-in through
`createDatabase({ temporal: { parse: true } })` and only applies to ORM-built
queries carrying column metadata, so raw SQL rows are left untouched.

**Acceptance criteria met.**

- Semantic SQL-to-Temporal column inference and explicit legacy modes.
- Temporal parameter serialization in ORM SQL rendering and direct adapter
  executors.
- Opt-in read parsing for selects, `returning()`, relational queries, and typed
  function calls.
- Drizzle parity, adapter compatibility docs, API reference, and migration notes
  updated.

---

## Tracking

When an item lands:

1. Implement it, add tests (and move the paired
   [drizzle-parity](drizzle-parity.md) row + parity test where noted).
2. Update the example to drop the corresponding raw-SQL workaround, and trim the
   matching entry in the example README's "Sisal API pressure points".
3. Flip the item's status here to 🟢 and record it under `Unreleased` in
   [CHANGELOG.md](../CHANGELOG.md).
```

### docs/v0.5.0-roadmap.md

URL: https://gilvandovieira.github.io/sisal/v0.5.0-roadmap.html

```md
---
title: v0.5.0 Roadmap
---

# Sisal v0.5.0 roadmap — driver parity & total test coverage

Where [v0.4.0](v0.4.0-roadmap.md) closed _feature_ gaps surfaced by the
`neon-hot-feed` example, v0.5.0 closes _parity_ gaps **between the adapters**.

**Mission.** Reach full feature parity across `@sisal/pg`, `@sisal/neon`,
`@sisal/sqlite`, and `@sisal/libsql` **wherever a dialect makes it possible**,
and cover **every** feature with an integration test on **every** adapter that
supports it. Where parity is _not_ possible (a feature is genuinely
Postgres-only), make the limitation explicit and graceful instead of silent.

**Outcome.** A single, generated **cross-driver feature matrix** in the docs
that replaces the four divergently-shaped per-engine matrices — one row per
feature, one column per adapter, every ✅ backed by a passing integration test.

## Where this came from

A parity audit (June 2026) compared the four compatibility matrices against the
_actual_ integration suites and the core render code, then ran real databases.
The asymmetry between offerings turned out to be **almost entirely missing test
coverage**, not missing capability:

- **SQLite** suite: 30/30 ✅ &nbsp; **libSQL** suite: 27/27 ✅ (both run
  first-hand).
- A throwaway probe ran the features the libSQL/Neon matrices _omit_ — column
  naming, keyset pagination, prepared statements, and `db.batch` (atomic commit
  **and** rollback) — against a real libSQL database: **5/5 passed**. These
  features share the exact render + driver paths that pg/sqlite already prove
  (`LIBSQL_DIALECT = "sqlite"`; Neon is `createPgOrmDriver` +
  `POSTGRES_DIALECT`).
- The same probe confirmed `.distinctOn()` is a **hard** limit on libSQL (the
  engine rejects the SQL), so the Postgres-only rows are correctly absent.

The three differences between offerings, only the first of which is a real
limit:

1. **Genuine dialect limits** — `distinctOn`, row locking (`.for(...)`), array
   operators (`@>`/`<@`/`&&`), the typed function caller (`db.call`), and
   `ilike` (degrades to `LIKE`). Postgres-family only; SQLite-family engines
   have no equivalent.
2. **Round-trip differences** — `json`/array as `TEXT`, `boolean` as `0/1`,
   `bytea` as `ArrayBuffer` on libSQL, `numeric`/`bigint` as numbers vs strings.
   The feature works; the value shape differs (marked ⚠️ today).
3. **Coverage gaps masquerading as imbalance** — column naming, keyset,
   prepared, `sql`-in-`SET`/`VALUES`, and `db.batch` work on every driver but
   are not tested on every driver. **This is the bulk of v0.5.0.**

## Status legend

| Symbol | Meaning                                |
| ------ | -------------------------------------- |
| 🔴     | Not started                            |
| 🟡     | In progress / partial                  |
| 🟢     | Done — move the entry to the CHANGELOG |

## Priority summary

| #  | Item                                                                   | Priority | Rough effort | Status |
| -- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------- | ------------ | ------ |
| 1  | libSQL integration coverage parity                                     | P1       | S            | 🟢     |
| 2  | Neon integration coverage parity                                       | P1       | M            | 🟢     |
| 3  | Unified cross-driver feature matrix (the **outcome**)                  | P1       | M            | 🟢     |
| 4  | Typed dialect guards for Postgres-only builders                        | P2       | M            | 🟢     |
| 5  | Document principled divergences & round-trip behavior                  | P2       | S            | 🟢     |
| 6  | Coverage-honesty: CHANGELOG fix + CI parity guard                      | P3       | S            | 🟢     |
| 7  | Richer DDL: functions & triggers (from v0.4.0 item 5)                  | P2       | L            | 🔴     |
| 8  | Portable atomic "transaction script" (procedure parity)                | P2       | L            | 🟡     |
| 9  | `FILTER` aggregates + interval/date math + `dateTrunc`                 | P2       | M            | 🔴     |
| 10 | `.optional()` should not widen the SELECT row type                     | P3       | S            | 🔴     |
| 11 | `@sisal/pg` float8 → `number` (only adapter returning string)          | P2       | M            | 🟢     |
| 12 | Data-modifying CTE builder (`WITH … INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE … RETURNING`) | P2       | L            | 🔴     |
| 13 | Typed raw-query result mapping (`db.query` row decoding)               | P3       | M            | 🔴     |

Items 8–13 were surfaced by the rising-feed example family (June 2026) — a
`/rising` moving-window feed built across engines and styles:
[`neon-rising-feed`](../examples/neon-rising-feed/README.md),
[`libsql-rising-feed`](../examples/libsql-rising-feed/README.md),
[`postgres-rising-feed`](../examples/postgres-rising-feed/README.md), and the
function-free
[`neon-rising-feed-ctes`](../examples/neon-rising-feed-ctes/README.md) (items
12–13). See [§ 8–13](#8-portable-atomic-transaction-script--p2) below.

P1 items 1–2 are the achievable-parity work and unblock item 3 (the matrix can
only claim a ✅ once a test backs it). Items 4–6 cover the parity that is _not_
achievable and keep the new matrix from rotting. Item 7 is separate — the DDL
function/trigger work deferred from v0.4.0, not part of the driver-parity
mission.

The per-adapter coverage gaps this roadmap closes (feature works, but no
integration test on that adapter):

| Feature                           | pg  | neon | sqlite | libsql |
| --------------------------------- | :-: | :--: | :----: | :----: |
| Column naming                     | ✅  |  ✅  |   ✅   |   ✅   |
| Keyset pagination                 | ✅  |  ✅  |   ✅   |   ✅   |
| Prepared statements               | ✅  |  ✅  |   ✅   |   ✅   |
| `sql` in `SET`/`VALUES`           | ✅  |  ✅  |   ✅   |   ✅   |
| `db.batch`                        | ✅  |  ✅  |   ✅   |   ✅   |
| Typed function caller (`db.call`) | ✅  |  ✅  |  n/a   |  n/a   |

→ **libSQL: 0 gaps (item 1 ✅). Neon: 0 gaps (item 2 ✅).** Both were coverage,
not capability. (Item 2 also ported **rich indexes**, a 7th pg-suite test the
Neon suite lacked beyond the six above.)

---

## 1. libSQL integration coverage parity — P1

**Problem.** `integration/libsql_features_test.ts` tests 26 features; the SQLite
suite tests three more that libSQL also supports but never exercises: **column
naming** (snake_case default / `.named()` / `preserve`), **keyset pagination**
(both predicate forms), and **prepared statements**. The
[libsql-compatibility](libsql-compatibility.md) matrix therefore _looks_
narrower than SQLite for no real reason. libSQL renders identical SQL to SQLite
(`LIBSQL_DIALECT = "sqlite"`), so these pass unchanged — verified directly by
the audit probe (5/5, including `db.batch` rollback).

**Proposed work.** Port the three SQLite tests into the libSQL suite (the
assertions are dialect-identical; only `connect`/teardown differ). They are a
near-mechanical copy of the existing `sqlite: column naming …`,
`sqlite: keyset pagination …`, and `sqlite: prepared statement …` tests.

**Affected.** `integration/libsql_features_test.ts`,
`docs/libsql-compatibility.md` (matrix rows + count `27 → 30`),
`docs/index.html` badge (`27/27 → 30/30`).

**Acceptance criteria.**

- `SISAL_LIBSQL_IT=1 deno test integration/libsql_features_test.ts` covers
  column naming, keyset (expanded + row-value), and prepared statements, all
  green on a local file and against Turso.
- The libSQL matrix gains the three rows and reads `30 / 30`.
- No new ⚠️ behavior notes are needed (these features round-trip identically).

---

## 2. Neon integration coverage parity — P1

**Problem.** `integration/neon_features_test.ts` covers 23 features; the pg
suite covers **six more** that Neon also supports — `db.batch`, `sql` in
`SET`/`VALUES`/`onConflict`, keyset pagination, prepared statements, column
naming, and the typed function caller — but the Neon suite tests none of them.
Neon is PostgreSQL reached through `createPgOrmDriver` + `POSTGRES_DIALECT`, so
the entire `@sisal/pg` surface is available; the gap is purely that the Neon
suite is slower to run (it needs a live endpoint or the Docker `neon-proxy`).

**Proposed work.** Add the six missing tests to the Neon suite (mirroring the pg
suite). Run them through the bundled `neon-proxy` so CI can exercise them
without a real Neon account, exactly as the existing Neon suite does.

**Affected.** `integration/neon_features_test.ts`, `docs/neon-compatibility.md`
(six matrix rows + run count), `docs/index.html` Neon badge.

**Acceptance criteria.**

- The Neon suite exercises batch, `sql`-in-`SET`/`VALUES`, keyset, prepared,
  column naming, and the typed function caller, green via `neon-proxy`.
- The Neon matrix reaches feature-row parity with the pg matrix (every
  non-Postgres-divergent row present).
- Alternatively, if a feature is deliberately left to inherit from `@sisal/pg`
  without re-verification, the matrix says so explicitly with a one-line reason
  (no silent omissions).

---

## 3. Unified cross-driver feature matrix — P1 — the **outcome**

**Problem.** Today there are four matrices
(`{pg,neon,sqlite,libsql}-compatibility.md`) with **different shapes** — Neon
omits rows pg has, libSQL omits rows SQLite has, and the homepage `#compat`
section aggregates them by hand. There is no single place a user can see "does
feature X work on driver Y, and is it tested?"

**Proposed work.** A single feature matrix — one row per feature, one column per
adapter — where every cell is `✅` (tested), `⚠️` (works, documented round-trip
difference), `❌` (genuine dialect limit, links to the reason), or `—` (n/a).
Generate it from a machine-readable source of truth so it cannot drift from the
suites, and surface it as the docs homepage `#compat` table plus a standalone
page. Target shape:

| Feature                              | pg  | neon | sqlite  |     libsql     |
| ------------------------------------ | :-: | :--: | :-----: | :------------: |
| Insert / update / delete / returning | ✅  |  ✅  |   ✅    |       ✅       |
| Filter / ordering / pagination       | ✅  |  ✅  |   ✅    |       ✅       |
| Joins (inner/left/right/full)        | ✅  |  ✅  |   ✅    |       ✅       |
| Aggregates / group / having          | ✅  |  ✅  |   ✅    |       ✅       |
| Subqueries / exists / CTE / set ops  | ✅  |  ✅  |   ✅    |       ✅       |
| Upsert (`onConflict…`)               | ✅  |  ✅  |   ✅    |       ✅       |
| `sql` in `SET`/`VALUES`              | ✅  |  ✅  |   ✅    |       ✅       |
| Column naming                        | ✅  |  ✅  |   ✅    |       ✅       |
| Keyset pagination                    | ✅  |  ✅  |   ✅    |       ✅       |
| Prepared statements                  | ✅  |  ✅  |   ✅    |       ✅       |
| Transactions                         | ✅  |  ✅  |   ✅    |       ✅       |
| `db.batch` (non-interactive)         | ✅  |  ✅  |   ✅    |       ✅       |
| Migrator (apply/plan/idempotent)     | ✅  |  ✅  |   ✅    |       ✅       |
| `ilike`                              | ✅  |  ✅  | ⚠️ LIKE |    ⚠️ LIKE     |
| `json`/array round-trip              | ✅  |  ✅  | ⚠️ text |    ⚠️ text     |
| `boolean` round-trip                 | ✅  |  ✅  | ⚠️ 0/1  |     ⚠️ 0/1     |
| `bytea`/BLOB round-trip              | ✅  |  ✅  |   ✅    | ⚠️ ArrayBuffer |
| `distinctOn`                         | ✅  |  ✅  |   ❌    |       ❌       |
| Row locking (`.for(...)`)            | ✅  |  ✅  |   ❌    |       ❌       |
| Array operators (`@>`/`<@`/`&&`)     | ✅  |  ✅  |   ❌    |       ❌       |
| Typed function caller (`db.call`)    | ✅  |  ✅  |   ❌    |       ❌       |

(The ✅ columns above are the _target_ once items 1–2 land; ❌/⚠️ rows are the
permanent, principled rows from items 4–5.)

**Status (🟢 done).** The generated matrix is live:
[`docs/feature-matrix.md`](feature-matrix.md) is rendered from a
machine-readable source of truth (`tools/feature_matrix.ts`) by
`tools/generate_feature_matrix.ts`, with `deno task docs:matrix` (regenerate)
and `docs:matrix:check` (verify). The check asserts **every ✅/⚠️ cell is backed
by a named integration test** in the matching suite — already the coverage-guard
half of item 6. 25 features × 4 adapters, 92 test-backed cells; regenerated by
the pre-commit hook and linked from the homepage `#compat`. All four per-engine
compatibility docs are trimmed to engine-specific metadata, behavior notes, and
reproduce steps + a link to the unified matrix (acceptance criterion 3) — their
divergent per-engine feature tables are retired.

**Affected.** A new `docs/feature-matrix.md` (or a generated section), the
homepage `docs/index.html` `#compat`, and the four per-engine docs (which can
then shrink to engine-specific _behavior notes_ and link to the unified matrix).

**Acceptance criteria.**

- One matrix shows every feature × every adapter with a single, consistent
  legend.
- Each `✅` corresponds to a named, passing integration test (ideally enforced —
  see item 6).
- The four per-engine docs keep only their behavior notes and reproduce steps,
  and link to the unified matrix instead of maintaining divergent feature lists.

---

## 4. Typed dialect guards for Postgres-only builders — P2

**Problem.** `.distinctOn(...)` (builders.ts) and `.for(...)` row locking render
**unconditionally** — there is no dialect guard. Calling them on a SQLite-family
database produces SQL the engine rejects, so misuse surfaces as a raw database
**syntax error** rather than a typed `OrmError` that names the real cause. The
audit confirmed this against live libSQL.

**Proposed work.** When a Postgres-only construct (`distinctOn`, `.for(...)`
locking, the array operators) is rendered against a non-Postgres dialect, throw
a clear `OrmError` (e.g.
`"distinctOn is PostgreSQL-only; the sqlite dialect has no
equivalent"`) at
render time, before execution. This is the "parity where it is _not_ possible"
half of the mission: the limit stays, but it becomes graceful and
self-explaining.

**Affected.** `@sisal/orm` (`builders.ts` render path, `errors.ts`), parity
tests in `packages/{sqlite}/drizzle_parity_test.ts`.

**Acceptance criteria.**

- Rendering `distinctOn` / `.for(...)` / array operators on a SQLite-family
  dialect throws a typed `OrmError` naming the construct and the dialect.
- Postgres/Neon behavior is unchanged.
- A unit test pins the thrown error for each construct per dialect.

---

## 5. Document principled divergences & round-trip behavior — P2

**Problem.** The genuine, permanent differences (Tier 1 limits and Tier 2
round-trip shapes from the audit) are described unevenly across the four docs.
The unified matrix (item 3) needs one canonical explanation to link its
`❌`/`⚠️` cells to.

**Proposed work.** A single "Driver capability & round-trip reference" section
that states, once: which constructs are Postgres-only and _why_ the
SQLite-family cannot offer them (no `ILIKE` keyword, no array type, no stored
functions, no row-level locking), and the canonical round-trip table
(`json`/array → `TEXT`, `boolean` → `0/1`, `bytea` → `ArrayBuffer` on libSQL,
`numeric`/`bigint` → number vs precision-string). The per-engine behavior notes
then reference it.

**Affected.** `docs/feature-matrix.md` (or a shared notes doc), the four
per-engine compatibility docs, [drizzle-parity](drizzle-parity.md) footnote ⁸
(already marks the array operators Postgres-only).

**Acceptance criteria.**

- Every `❌` and `⚠️` cell in the unified matrix links to a one-paragraph
  reason.
- No conflicting or duplicated explanations remain across the per-engine docs.

---

## 6. Coverage-honesty: CHANGELOG fix + CI parity guard — P3

**Problem.** Two honesty issues the audit found. (a) The `Unreleased` `db.batch`
CHANGELOG entry says it is _"Covered by … the gated integration suites"_, but
the **Neon** suite has no batch test — the claim overstates current coverage.
(b) Nothing stops the matrices from drifting from the suites again.

**Proposed work.** Correct the `db.batch` changelog wording to name the suites
that actually cover it (pg/sqlite/libsql) until item 2 lands, then update it.
Add a lightweight check (extending `tools/` or the integration workflow) that
parses the unified matrix's `✅` cells and asserts a correspondingly-named test
exists in the matching suite — failing CI when a matrix claims coverage that no
test backs (mirroring how the drizzle-parity tests pin that doc).

**Affected.** `CHANGELOG.md`, `tools/` (new parity-coverage check),
`.github/workflows` integration job, the unified matrix's source of truth.

**Acceptance criteria.**

- The `db.batch` changelog line matches reality.
- A CI check fails if the unified matrix marks a feature ✅ for an adapter whose
  suite has no corresponding test.
- The check is documented next to `docs:check` in CLAUDE.md's commands list.

---

## 7. Richer DDL: functions & triggers — P2 (deferred from v0.4.0 item 5)

**Context.** [v0.4.0 item 5](v0.4.0-roadmap.md) shipped richer **index** DDL
(per-column `ASC`/`DESC`, partial `WHERE`, and `` sql`...` `` expression keys)
and already emitted `CHECK` constraints inline. The remaining sub-item — 5.3,
explicitly the "stretch" — is **functions and triggers**, which is a larger
design than an index tweak and so moves here.

**Problem.** The DDL generators emit only schema objects derived from
`defineTable` (tables, columns, constraints, indexes). They cannot express a
stored `CREATE FUNCTION` / `CREATE TRIGGER` body, so an app like
`examples/neon-hot-feed` keeps its `app.vote_post` function and hot-score
function as hand-written `.sql` migrations and treats `src/schema.ts` as a typed
mirror rather than the generator's source of truth.

**Proposed work.** A way to attach raw, dialect-specific DDL fragments
(functions, triggers, extensions) to a schema snapshot so generated migrations
include them verbatim, ordered **after** table/column/constraint creation. This
needs design for: ordering relative to tables, `down`/drop generation,
drift/checksum handling for opaque SQL bodies, and per-dialect gating (Postgres
functions vs. SQLite triggers differ).

**Affected.** `@sisal/orm` (`schema.ts` snapshot — a new
`functions`/`schemaObjects` array; `table.ts` or a new `defineSchemaObject`
helper), each adapter DDL generator, and `@sisal/migrate` diff classification.

**Acceptance criteria.**

- A snapshot can carry ordered raw DDL fragments emitted after table creation,
  with matching drop statements for `down`.
- `examples/neon-hot-feed` can regenerate its full `0001_init.sql` (functions
  included) from `src/schema.ts`.
- Drift detection accounts for changed function/trigger bodies.
- Move the corresponding [drizzle-parity](drizzle-parity.md) rows.

---

## 8. Portable atomic "transaction script" — P2 (procedure parity)

**Context.** The [`neon-rising-feed`](../examples/neon-rising-feed/README.md) ↔
[`libsql-rising-feed`](../examples/libsql-rising-feed/README.md) pair builds the
identical `/rising` feed on both engines. The activity recorder — dedupe the
actor, bump a counter, bump `unique_actors` only on the actor's first touch,
recompute the bucket score — is the same logic, but it reads **completely
differently** per engine because SQLite has no stored procedures:

- **Neon:** one `app.record_post_activity(...)` function, called as a single
  atomic statement through the typed `db.call` surface — Deno-Deploy friendly.
- **libSQL:** the same steps reimplemented in TypeScript inside an interactive
  `db.transaction(...)` (`insert().onConflictDoNothing().returning()` to detect
  a new actor, an `onConflictDoUpdate` upsert with raw-`sql` increments, a
  `select()` readback).

Both are clean, but the application code is duplicated and shaped by the engine,
not the domain.

**Problem.** There is no Sisal-level way to express "run these steps atomically"
that compiles to a **database function call where one exists** and an
**interactive transaction otherwise**. `db.batch` is close but forbids a
statement depending on a previous one's result, which this read-modify-write
needs.

**Proposed work (design).** A "transaction script" / atomic-operation helper:
author the steps once against the builder; on Postgres-family adapters it can be
backed by (or generated into) a `CREATE FUNCTION` and invoked as one statement,
while SQLite-family adapters run it as an interactive transaction. Needs design
for: how dependent results flow between steps, how it interacts with item 7
(function DDL generation), and the serverless single-round-trip guarantee.

**Affected.** `@sisal/orm` (new API + render/execute paths), every adapter, and
item 7's DDL generation. **Acceptance:** the rising-feed recorder reads
identically in both examples; it is one round trip on Neon HTTP and one
interactive transaction on libSQL; covered on every adapter.

**Status (🟡 in progress).** The uniform path landed: `defineAtomicOperation`
(`@sisal/orm`) authors dependent read-modify-write steps once and runs them as
one **interactive transaction on every adapter** — covered by
`packages/orm/atomic_test.ts` (network-free) + an `atomic operation` integration
test in all four suites and a unified-matrix row. **Deferred:** the
**single-round-trip Postgres path** (dispatch to a generated function or a
data-modifying CTE — items 7/12) and the rising-feed example unification (the
examples still showcase their engine-optimal patterns until the function path
exists). The authored body is forward-compatible with that dispatch.

---

## 9. `FILTER` aggregates + interval/date math + `dateTrunc` — P2

**Problem.** The moving-window score is a conditional aggregate over time
windows:

```sql
sum(activity_score) FILTER (WHERE bucket_start >= p_now - interval '15 minutes')
```

Sisal's builder has `sum`/`count`/`avg`/… but **no `FILTER (WHERE …)` clause**,
**no interval/date arithmetic**, and **no `dateTrunc`/time-bucket helper**. So
in `neon-rising-feed` the whole score lives in a raw SQL function, and in
`libsql-rising-feed` it is computed in TypeScript after reading the buckets —
and the 5-minute bucket flooring is hand-written per dialect (`app.bucket_5m` in
SQL, `bucket5mIso` in TS).

**Proposed work.** (a) A `filter(condition)` modifier on aggregate expressions
that renders `agg(...) FILTER (WHERE ...)` (Postgres) and degrades to
`agg(CASE WHEN ... THEN ... END)` on SQLite. (b) An interval/`now`-relative date
helper for `where`/expressions. (c) A portable `dateTrunc('5 minutes', col)` /
bucket helper that renders per dialect (`date_trunc` /
`to_timestamp(floor(...))` on PG, `unixepoch`/`strftime` math on SQLite).

**Affected.** `@sisal/orm` (`operators.ts`, `sql.ts`, render paths), adapter
dialect rendering. **Acceptance:** the rising-score window query is
builder-native on both engines; render tests pin the per-dialect SQL; the
examples drop their raw-SQL/TS-only window math.

---

## 10. `.optional()` should not widen the SELECT row type — P3

**Problem.** `.optional()` is documented as an **insert-only** axis that "does
not change column nullability." But marking a nullable column `.optional()` adds
`undefined` to its inferred **SELECT** type (`Date | null | undefined`), so a
plain `InferSelect` row no longer matches a hand-written `T | null` interface.
Surfaced in `neon-rising-feed`: `rising_score_updated_at` had to stay
required-on-insert (passing an explicit `null`) to keep the feed row type clean.

**Proposed work.** Make `.optional()` affect only `InferInsert` (the insert
optionality), leaving `InferSelect` as `T | null`. **Affected.** `@sisal/orm`
column/type inference. **Acceptance:** an `.optional()` nullable column infers
`T | null` on select and `T | null | undefined`-omittable on insert; a type test
pins both; the example can use `.optional()` without widening the feed row.

---

## 11. Coerce numeric reads to `number` (float8 returns as string) — P2

**Context.** Surfaced while validating the
[`neon-rising-feed`](../examples/neon-rising-feed/README.md) example against
real databases (June 2026). **`@sisal/pg` is the only adapter that returns
`double precision` as a string** — a driver-parity gap, which is exactly this
roadmap's theme. Verified first-hand, all reading the same
`306.0::double
precision`:

| Adapter         | `double precision` read | Notes                                        |
| --------------- | ----------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| `@sisal/pg`     | `"306"` (**string**)    | `jsr:@db/postgres` decodes `float8` → string |
| `@sisal/neon`   | `306` (number)          | `@neon/serverless` decodes `float8` → number |
| `@sisal/sqlite` | `306` (number)          | SQLite `REAL` → number                       |
| `@sisal/libsql` | `306` (number)          | SQLite `REAL` → number                       |

Integers (`int2`/`int4`) come back as numbers on `@sisal/pg`, and `timestamptz`
maps to `Temporal`/`Date`, so only the float types are inconsistent.

**Problem.** A column declared `columns.doublePrecision()` infers `number`, but
on `@sisal/pg` the runtime value is a `string`. This is a silent type lie with
real consequences:

- client-side numeric work breaks — `score.toFixed(2)` throws (`"306".toFixed`
  is not a function), and `Array.sort`/comparisons run lexicographically
  (`"180" < "72"` is `true`);
- a keyset/ORDER BY whose tiebreaker is a float column still _orders correctly
  in the database_ (the DB casts the bound string param), so the bug hides until
  a consumer touches the value in JS.

**Where it surfaced.** Running the `neon-rising-feed` TypeScript path against a
local PostgreSQL 18 via `@sisal/pg`: `rising_score` came back as a string, so
the feed's `non-increasing` ordering check failed lexicographically and
`rising_score.toFixed(...)` would throw. The example itself uses
**`@sisal/neon`** (which returns a number), so it is _not_ affected and was left
uncoerced; but `@sisal/pg` (and `neon-hot-feed`'s `hot_score`, if run through
`@sisal/pg`) is.

**Proposed work.** Decode `float4`/`float8` to `number` in the `@sisal/pg`
result path so a `columns.doublePrecision` column reads back as the `number` it
infers, matching the other three adapters. `numeric`/`bigint` stay strings by
necessity (precision) and belong in the round-trip reference (item 5); this item
is specifically about the float types, where `number` is the correct, lossless
mapping.

**Affected.** `@sisal/pg` result decoding (`jsr:@db/postgres` type parsers) only
— `@sisal/neon` already returns a number;
[round-trip reference](#5-document-principled-divergences--round-trip-behavior-p2)
(item 5); the unified matrix (item 3) gains a tested "float round-trip" row.

**Acceptance criteria.**

- A `double precision` column read through `@sisal/pg` yields a JS `number`,
  matching `@sisal/neon` / `@sisal/sqlite` / `@sisal/libsql`.
- Integration coverage on pg asserts the runtime `typeof` is `number`.
- The float round-trip is added to the round-trip reference (item 5) and the
  unified matrix (item 3).

---

## 12. Data-modifying CTE builder (`WITH … INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE … RETURNING`) — P2

**Context.** Surfaced by
[`neon-rising-feed-ctes`](../examples/neon-rising-feed-ctes/README.md) (June
2026) — a deliberately function-free variant of the rising feed that models
every multi-step mutation as **one data-modifying CTE statement** (Neon
HTTP-friendly: one parameterized statement, no interactive transaction
callback). The activity recorder is a
`WITH input_data → validated_input →
bucket_data → actor_insert(INSERT…ON CONFLICT…RETURNING) → actor_flag →
bucket_upsert(INSERT…ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE…RETURNING)`
statement; the recompute paths are
`WITH score_windows → computed_score → updated_post(UPDATE…FROM…
RETURNING)`.

**Problem.** Sisal's CTE builder is **SELECT-only**: `db.$with(name).as(query)`
takes a `SetOperand` (a `SELECT`/compound) and `db.with(...ctes)` terminates in
`.select(...)`. There is no way to:

- use an `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE … RETURNING` as a CTE body,
- chain such stages (one CTE consuming another's `RETURNING`),
- or return rows from a `WITH`-wrapped data-modifying statement.

So the entire recorder and both recompute statements are hand-written raw `sql`
template strings — by far the largest raw-SQL surface in any example. The
builder's `db.batch` (independent statements) and interactive `transaction`
(multiple round trips / open session) do **not** cover the "several dependent
steps as one atomic statement" shape, which is exactly what data-modifying CTEs
give and what the serverless story wants.

**Where it surfaced.** `examples/neon-rising-feed-ctes/src/activity.ts`
(`recordPostActivity`) and `src/rising.ts` (`recomputePostRisingScore`,
`recomputeAllRisingScores`) — all raw `sql`.

**Proposed work (design).** A composable data-modifying CTE builder, e.g.
`db.$with("actor_insert").as(db.insert(actors).values(...).onConflictDoNothing().returning())`,
allowing CTE bodies to be insert/update/delete builders, downstream CTEs to
reference upstream `RETURNING` columns, and the chain to terminate in a final
`SELECT`/`RETURNING` whose row type is inferred. This is a large design (CTE
dependency typing, `EXCLUDED`/`ON CONFLICT` interplay, dialect support —
Postgres yes, SQLite supports data-modifying-CTE less fully) and overlaps with
item 8 (portable atomic operations).

**Affected.** `@sisal/orm` (`builders.ts` CTE types + `database.ts`
`$with`/`with` to accept mutation operands and a mutating terminal), each
adapter's render path.

**Acceptance criteria.**

- A `WITH` chain whose stages are `INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE … RETURNING` builders
  can be assembled and executed, returning a typed row.
- The `neon-rising-feed-ctes` recorder + recompute can drop to the builder (or
  the example documents precisely which sub-feature still needs raw SQL).
- Render tests pin the generated `WITH` SQL and parameter order.

---

## 13. Typed raw-query result mapping — P3

**Context.** Also from
[`neon-rising-feed-ctes`](../examples/neon-rising-feed-ctes/README.md): because
the mutations are raw `sql` (item 12), their result rows come back through
`db.query<T>(sql\`…\`)`, which **trusts the caller's`<T>`generic** and does no
column-metadata-driven decoding. Each CTE's shape is hand-written
(`RecordedBucket`,`RecomputedPost`)
and trusted to match the SQL.

**Problem.** Raw-query rows are driver-shaped with no `defineTable`-driven
mapping: there is no way to say "decode this raw result as
`post_activity_buckets` rows" and get column naming, date/number coercion
(relates to item 11), and a checked row type. The hand-typed generic can
silently drift from the SQL, and on `@sisal/pg` the `double precision` columns
even come back as strings (item 11) with no typed layer to fix it.

**Proposed work.** A typed raw-result mapper — e.g.
`db.query(sql\`…\`).as(post_activity_buckets)`or`sql<typeof bucketRowShape>\`…\``— that applies a column descriptor map to a raw
result: physical→JS key naming, per-column decoding (float8→number, timestamptz→
Date/Temporal), and a derived row type. Pairs naturally with item 11 (float
decoding) and item 12 (typing a data-modifying CTE's`RETURNING`).

**Affected.** `@sisal/orm` (`database.ts` query surface + a result-metadata
mapper reusing the column descriptors `defineFunction`/select already carry).

**Acceptance criteria.**

- A raw query result can be decoded against a column-descriptor map, yielding a
  typed row with consistent naming and value coercion across adapters.
- The CTE example's hand-written `RecordedBucket` / `RecomputedPost` shapes can
  be derived from the table models instead of restated.

---

## Tracking

When an item lands:

1. Implement it, add the integration tests on **every** supporting adapter, and
   move the paired [drizzle-parity](drizzle-parity.md) row where noted.
2. Update the unified feature matrix (item 3) and retire the corresponding rows
   from the per-engine docs.
3. Flip the item's status here to 🟢 and record it under `Unreleased` in
   [CHANGELOG.md](../CHANGELOG.md).

The roadmap is complete when the unified matrix shows, for every feature, either
a tested `✅` on every supporting adapter or a documented `❌`/`⚠️`/`—` with a
linked reason — and CI enforces that every `✅` is backed by a test.
```

### docs/v0.6.0-roadmap.md

URL: https://gilvandovieira.github.io/sisal/v0.6.0-roadmap.html

```md
---
title: v0.6.0 Roadmap
---

# Sisal v0.6.0 roadmap — Foundations & Readiness

Where [v0.5.0](v0.5.0-roadmap.md) makes the OLTP builder boringly correct across
the four dialects, v0.6.0 is the **readiness** milestone: it earns the right to
build later packages (and reach a fifth dialect) by proving the foundation can
carry them. It is deliberately **three parallel workstreams**, all of which
_prepare the ground and ship no new public feature package_:

- **Workstream A — ETL Readiness Investigation.** The minimum ORM/core changes
  so a future `@sisal/etl` (v0.10) can be built without hacks.
- **Workstream B — Node.js + npm Runtime Readiness.** Make the packages
  installable/runnable on Node 24+ and publishable to npm alongside JSR, without
  giving up the Deno workflow. (This is the runtime/packaging track folded into
  v0.6; see [roadmap overview](roadmap.md).)
- **Workstream C — MySQL Support Investigation.** Map what a `@sisal/mysql`
  adapter ([v0.7](v0.7.0-roadmap.md) builds it) needs, given that the renderer
  already carries a latent `"mysql"` dialect. Investigation only — no adapter.

**This release builds nothing it investigates.** v0.6 answers "can Sisal express
ETL-shaped SQL cleanly?" and "what does MySQL support require?" — it ships
neither the ETL package nor the MySQL adapter. See
[release discipline](roadmap.md#release-discipline-non-negotiable).

## Status legend

| Symbol | Meaning                                |
| ------ | -------------------------------------- |
| 🔴     | Not started                            |
| 🟡     | In progress / partial                  |
| 🟢     | Done — move the entry to the CHANGELOG |

---

# Workstream A — ETL Readiness Investigation

## Goal

Investigate and implement **only** the minimum ORM/core changes needed so a
future `@sisal/etl` package can express extract→transform→load as pushed-down
SQL without dropping to raw strings for the core shapes. Produce a **dialect
capability matrix** and a **proof-of-concept rollup** in docs/pseudo-code.

## Questions this release must answer

- Can Sisal express ETL-shaped SQL (grouped rollup → idempotent upsert) cleanly
  through the typed builder?
- Does the current internal query representation support this, or does it need a
  stronger SQL IR/AST? (See the [v0.8](v0.8.0-roadmap.md) IR work it feeds.)
- Can each dialect (pg/neon/sqlite/libsql) support the required SQL patterns?
- Which features belong in **core** and which belong in **dialect adapters**?
- What must be postponed to v0.8/v0.10?

## Scope — investigate & document support for

Set-based movement and aggregation (the rollup spine):

- `INSERT INTO … SELECT …` (insert-from-select) — **today: absent** (the insert
  builder takes only client-side literals). The single biggest ETL gap.
- CTEs (`WITH`) — **today: SELECT-only**; data-modifying CTEs are
  [v0.5 item 12](v0.5.0-roadmap.md).
- Aggregate expressions; `GROUP BY`; `HAVING` — **today: supported**.
- Conditional aggregation: `COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE …)` where available, with a
  `SUM(CASE WHEN … THEN … ELSE … END)` fallback where `FILTER` is unavailable
  (SQLite/libSQL) — **today: absent**, overlaps
  [v0.5 item 9](v0.5.0-roadmap.md).
- Upsert from select (`INSERT … SELECT … ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE`) — **today:
  absent** (upsert exists for literal values only).
- Typed raw-SQL escape hatches — **today: present** (`sql` tag binds params
  safely); the fallback for anything unbuilt.
- Computed expressions and expression **aliases** in projections — partial;
  needs a first-class typed alias surface for rollup metric columns.
- Date/time **bucket** abstraction (`date_trunc('hour', …)` and friends) —
  **today: absent**, [v0.5 item 9](v0.5.0-roadmap.md).

Execution & correctness primitives the runner will need:

- Batch execution; transaction boundaries — **today: supported** (`db.batch`,
  `db.transaction`). Note `db.batch` forbids cross-statement data dependencies.
- **Advisory locks or an equivalent locking strategy** — Postgres has
  `pg_advisory_lock` (already used internally by the migration history store — a
  reusable pattern); SQLite/libSQL need an equivalent (a lock row /
  `BEGIN
  IMMEDIATE`). Document the per-dialect strategy.
- **Checkpoint table design** — a watermark table (`job`, `window_end`,
  `updated_at`) so a run resumes from the last committed window.
- **Idempotent load strategy** — re-running a window must not double-count
  (upsert/replace semantics keyed by the rollup grain).
- **Backfill/replay requirements** — re-run an arbitrary historical window range
  deterministically.

## Deliverable — dialect capability matrix

A matrix across **PostgreSQL · Neon · SQLite · libSQL** covering at least:
insert-from-select, upsert, returning, CTEs, window functions, filtered
aggregates, materialized views, advisory locks, date-bucket support, transaction
behavior, serverless/runtime caveats. The audited starting point:

| Capability           | pg                         | neon                                                  | sqlite                              | libsql                                  |
| -------------------- | -------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| insert-from-select   | engine ✅ / **no builder** | engine ✅ / **no builder**                            | needs-verification / no builder     | needs-verification / no builder         |
| upsert (ON CONFLICT) | ✅                         | ✅                                                    | ✅                                  | ✅                                      |
| returning            | ✅                         | ✅                                                    | ✅                                  | ✅                                      |
| CTEs (`WITH`)        | ✅ (builder, SELECT-only)  | ✅                                                    | render-only, **no per-engine test** | render-only, **no per-engine test**     |
| data-modifying CTEs  | engine ✅ / unbuilt        | engine ✅ / unbuilt                                   | "less fully" / unbuilt              | "less fully" / unbuilt                  |
| window functions     | engine ✅ / **no builder** | engine ✅ / **no builder**                            | engine ✅ / no builder              | engine ✅ / no builder                  |
| filtered aggregates  | planned (item 9)           | planned                                               | degrade→CASE (item 9)               | degrade→CASE                            |
| materialized views   | engine ✅ / no DDL         | engine ✅ / no DDL                                    | ❌ none                             | ❌ none                                 |
| advisory locks       | ✅ (pg_advisory_lock)      | ✅                                                    | ❌ (use lock row)                   | ❌ (use lock row)                       |
| date bucketing       | planned (item 9)           | planned                                               | planned (unixepoch)                 | planned                                 |
| transactions         | interactive ✅             | interactive ✅ (WS Pool)                              | interactive ✅                      | interactive ✅                          |
| serverless caveats   | —                          | WebSocket Pool; favor single-statement; float8→number | embedded/FFI                        | Turso remote (hrana); ArrayBuffer BLOBs |

(Cells marked "no builder" work via the `sql` escape hatch today; the readiness
question is which deserve a typed builder in v0.8 vs stay raw.)

## Deliverable — proof-of-concept rollup (docs / pseudo-code only)

A `post_events → post_hourly_stats` rollup demonstrating the desired shape (the
repo's existing `*-rising-feed` examples already bucket activity — this
generalizes that into an ETL rollup):

- **Extract** from a raw `post_events` table over a half-open time window
  `[from, until)`.
- **Group** by `post_id` and an hourly bucket.
- **Compute** `views`, `votes`, `comments`, `engagement_score`.
- **Load** into `post_hourly_stats` with idempotent upsert/replace.
- **Checkpoint** the window end (kept as a _future concern_ — design, don't
  ship).

The key SQL shape v0.6 must establish a path to express:

```sql
INSERT INTO post_hourly_stats (post_id, bucket, views, votes, comments, engagement_score)
SELECT post_id,
       date_trunc('hour', occurred_at) AS bucket,
       count(*) FILTER (WHERE kind = 'view')    AS views,
       count(*) FILTER (WHERE kind = 'vote')    AS votes,
       count(*) FILTER (WHERE kind = 'comment') AS comments,
       /* weighted */                            AS engagement_score
FROM post_events
WHERE occurred_at >= $from AND occurred_at < $until
GROUP BY post_id, date_trunc('hour', occurred_at)
ON CONFLICT (post_id, bucket)
DO UPDATE SET views = excluded.views, votes = excluded.votes,
              comments = excluded.comments, engagement_score = excluded.engagement_score;
```

This single statement exercises every audited gap: insert-from-select, a date
bucket, filtered aggregates (with the SQLite `CASE WHEN` fallback), and
upsert-from-select. v0.6 decides which of these become typed builders in v0.8.

## Workstream A non-goals

No public `@sisal/etl` package; no scheduler; no worker daemon; no Rust/native
engine; no DuckDB adapter; no dashboard API; no full analytics module.

---

# Workstream B — Node.js + npm Runtime Readiness

Make every package install and run on **Node.js 24+** from **npm** and
dual-publish JSR + npm, while the Deno workflow stays exactly as it is today.
The library cores already run under Node's type-stripping; the blockers are
packaging metadata, the six `jsr:`/`npm:` import sites, and the `.ts` import
extensions — not the library logic. Items 1–8 below carry the full detail.

**Package naming (JSR `@sisal` vs npm `@scope`).** The `@sisal` scope is
published and owned on **JSR**, but is **unavailable on npm**. So the Deno
workspace, every source import, and the JSR packages keep the `@sisal/*` names
unchanged, while the npm artifacts publish under a **placeholder scope written
`@scope/*`** throughout this workstream. The final npm name is an open branding
decision (an alternate scope like `@sisal-db/*`, unscoped `sisal-*` packages,
your own scope, …); `@scope` is a deliberate stand-in so the work is not tied to
a name — swap it in one find-replace once chosen. Mechanically, the npm **build
remaps** the source `@sisal/*` scope to `@scope/*` (package `name`, dependency
